description:
You are an operations engineer who has just resolved a minor production incident for a small web service located in  
  /home/user/app.  
  The engineering policy requires you to:

  1. Perform a patch-level semantic version bump (e.g., 2.3.4 → 2.3.5) in **exactly one** place: /home/user/app/VERSION.
  2. Add a brand-new top-most section to the existing changelog file /home/user/app/CHANGELOG.md that documents  
     a) the new version number,  
     b) the release date in ISO-8601 format (YYYY-MM-DD), and  
     c) a concise one-line description of the incident fix.  
  3. Create a lightweight Git tag that matches the new version in the form vX.Y.Z.
  4. Produce a separate machine-readable audit file called /home/user/app/.release_log that summarises what you did.

  Exact details you must observe:

  A. VERSION  
     • The file contains nothing but a single semantic version string followed by a single trailing newline.  
     • After you finish, that string must be “2.3.5”.

  B. CHANGELOG.md  
     • Pre-existing sections already follow the template of “## <version> – <date>” followed by one or more bullet points.  
     • Your new section **must be inserted at the very top of the file** using the same template.  
     • Example of the required syntax (you must replace the date):  
       ```
       ## 2.3.5 – 2024-03-15
       * Fix: Resolved Redis connection leak that caused elevated 500 errors.
       ```  
       (One blank line must follow the bullet list before the next “##” header.)

  C. Git tag  
     • The tag must be named v2.3.5 (note the leading “v”).  
     • The tag’s message can stay empty (lightweight tag).

  D. .release_log  
     • Plain-text file, three lines only, **no extra whitespace**:  
       1. NewVersion: 2.3.5  
       2. Tagged: v2.3.5  
       3. Date: YYYY-MM-DD   (same date you used in the changelog)

  Once done, the automated checks will look for:  
  • /home/user/app/VERSION containing “2.3.5\n” exactly.  
  • /home/user/app/CHANGELOG.md whose first non-blank line is “## 2.3.5 – <date>”.  
  • git –C /home/user/app tag --list must output v2.3.5.  
  • /home/user/app/.release_log with the three lines described above.

  You have no root access and start in /home/user. Typical command count to finish: 5-10 shell commands.
truth:
Initial repository layout:

  /home/user/app/.git                  (a fully initialised git repository)
  /home/user/app/VERSION               contents: "2.3.4\n"
  /home/user/app/CHANGELOG.md          contents:
    "
    ## 2.3.4 – 2024-02-10
    * Fix: Updated dependency versions.

    ## 2.3.3 – 2024-02-01
    * Change: Improved logging around authentication.
    "
  (there may be more historical sections below; order newest→oldest)

  The starting Git repository already has an initial commit that tracks both files
  and has the tag v2.3.4 pointing at HEAD.

  Expected final state to be verified:

  1. /home/user/app/VERSION
     exact bytes → "2.3.5\n"

  2. /home/user/app/CHANGELOG.md
     first 3 logical lines →
       "## 2.3.5 – 2024-03-15"          (any date accepted by test harness, captured dynamically)
       "* Fix: Resolved Redis connection leak that caused elevated 500 errors."
       ""                               (blank line)
     after this, the old “## 2.3.4 – …” section remains unchanged.

  3. A lightweight Git tag called v2.3.5 must exist and point at the same commit
     that updated VERSION and CHANGELOG.md.

  4. /home/user/app/.release_log
     must contain exactly:
       "NewVersion: 2.3.5\nTagged: v2.3.5\nDate: 2024-03-15\n"
     (the date line must match the date that appears in the changelog header).

  No other files should be changed.
